claims.claim_text: REopt.jl formulates distributed energy resource (DER) sizing and dispatch as a single-objective mixed-integer linear program (MILP) whose scalar objective minimizes the life-cycle cost (LCC) of energy over a user-specified analysis horizon.

The net present value (NPV) is defined as the difference between the business-as-usual (BAU) LCC and the optimized-case LCC, so minimizing LCC and maximizing NPV are algebraically equivalent; no Pareto multi-objective frontier is constructed.
The objective function assembles additive cost terms, each discounted to present value using the off-taker's nominal discount rate over `analysis_years` (default 25 years).
For each technology t in subdivision k, segment s, the capital cost is represented by a piecewise-linear function with slope c^{cm}_{ts} [$/kW] and intercept c^{cb}_{ts} [$].
Storage systems contribute two independent capital cost terms: c^{kW}_b [$/kW] for power capacity and c^{kWh}_b [$/kWh] for energy capacity.
Battery replacement or augmentation costs C_{repl} or C_{aug} are added to the objective when `model_degradation` is enabled.
source_chunk_ids: Inputs · REopt.jl Documentation_The replacement strategy cost is:_0_20

claims.claim_text: Both fixed O&M per unit of power rating (c^{omσ}_t [$/kW], which includes standby charges for CHP) and variable O&M per unit of production (c^{omp}_t [$/kWh]) are discounted over the analysis period using the present worth factor f_{om}.

Electricity purchases are valued at time-of-use energy prices c^{g}_{uh} [$/kWh] across tiered demand structures. Demand charges enter via monthly peak demand tiers (c^{rm}_{mn} [$/kW]) and time-of-use demand periods (c^{r}_{de} [$/kW]), with ratchet provisions over look-back months.
Fuel costs for on-site generation enter at c^{u}_f [$/MMBTU] discounted by technology-specific present worth factors f^{pf}_t.

When enabled, marginal costs of pollutant p from on-site fuel burn (c^{f}_p [$/ton]) and from grid-purchased electricity (c̄^{g}_p [$/ton]) are included, each with their own present worth factors f^{fc}_p and f^{gc}_p.
The expected outage cost term is weighted by `value_of_lost_load_per_kwh` (VoLL) and `outage_probabilities`, penalizing unserved energy during stochastic outage scenarios.
Energy export revenues at rates c^{e}_{uh} [$/kWh] across net-metering and wholesale tiers are subtracted from cost in the objective.
Production incentives X^{pi}_t [$] are bounded by technology-specific caps ī_t and power limits ī^{σ}_t.

The ITC reduces CAPEX directly (e.g., 30% for PV and storage meeting prevailing wage requirements), while MACRS bonus depreciation (up to 100% first-year under current law) reduces the depreciation basis by half the ITC value.
Battery residual value C_{residual}, computed as the present worth of remaining useful life at the end of the analysis period, is subtracted from total cost.
source_chunk_ids: Inputs · REopt.jl Documentation_The replacement strategy cost is:_0_20

claims.claim_text: ISO demand response (DR) capacity revenue is not native to the objective but is computed in post-processing, derived from the solved `ElectricStorage.size_kw` and `Generator.size_kw` capacities after the optimization terminates, and is reported as an ancillary economic metric rather than influencing DER sizing directly.
NPV is calculated as NPV = LCC_{BAU} - LCC_{Optimal}.
The BAU case includes only utility demand and energy costs, existing boiler fuel costs, and O&M for any existing PV or generator.
The optimized case adds CAPEX, tax benefits, incentives, and O&M for all recommended DER.
Fixed utility fees cancel in the NPV calculation because they cannot be offset by DER.

For financial analyses, NPV is guaranteed non-negative unless the user forces a minimum technology size; for resilience analyses, NPV may be negative, indicating that the resilience premium exceeds the economic benefit.
Avoided outage costs are not included in the NPV calculation by default, though adding them may increase NPV.
The stochastic outage formulation evaluates resilience across a combinatorial grid of `outage_start_time_steps` and `outage_durations`, each weighted by `outage_probabilities`.

The expected outage cost takes the form: ExpectedOutageCost = Σ_{s∈S} p_s Σ_{t∈T_s} VoLL_t · UnservedLoad_{s,t}, where p_s is the probability of outage scenario s and the inner summation runs over the time steps t within that scenario's duration.

The critical structural feature is a minimax arrangement: the maximum is taken over `outage_start_time_steps` (the optimizer must hedge against the worst-case start time), while the expectation is taken over `outage_durations` weighted by `outage_probabilities`.
Outage time-series results are stored as three-dimensional arrays indexed by [duration_scenario, start_time_step, timestep_within_outage].

The scenario JSON is loaded via `JSON.parsefile("path/to/scenario.json")`, which returns a nested `JSON.Object`. A documented bug requires recursive conversion of these objects to native Julia `Dict` types before passing them to REopt internals.
source_chunk_ids: 1. Structure of the REopt API · NatLabRockies:REopt_API Wiki_Django Models and Input Validation_0_57

claims.claim_text: The JuMP model is constructed with `m = Model(HiGHS.Optimizer)` and solver-level attributes are configured via `set_attribute(m, "time_limit", seconds)`.
source_chunk_ids: 1. Structure of the REopt API · NatLabRockies:REopt_API Wiki_Django Models and Input Validation_0_57

claims.claim_text: The REopt User Manual specifies default optimality tolerances of 0.1% for general cases, 1% for CHP scenarios, and 5% for off-grid analyses.

`run_reopt` supports two calling signatures: a single-model signature `results = run_reopt(m, scenario_dict)` that solves the optimized case only, and a two-model signature `results = run_reopt(m1, m2, scenario_dict)` that solves both the BAU and optimized cases in the same invocation, enabling automatic NPV calculation.
source_chunk_ids: 1. Structure of the REopt API · NatLabRockies:REopt_API Wiki_Django Models and Input Validation_0_57
1. Structure of the REopt API · NatLabRockies:REopt_API Wiki_The steps behind an optimization are:_0_56

claims.claim_text: The v3 architecture runs BAU and optimal scenarios in parallel within the Julia process, consolidating what were previously four separate Celery tasks into one.
source_chunk_ids: 1. Structure of the REopt API · NatLabRockies:REopt_API Wiki_Django Models and Input Validation_0_57

claims.claim_text: REopt returns results containing `JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray` objects, which are not directly JSON-serializable. A recursive `make_serializable` function must traverse the results dictionary, converting every `DenseAxisArray` to a standard Julia `Array` before calling `JSON.print(io, results)`.

Parametric studies such as sweeping `Financial.value_of_lost_load_per_kwh` follow a standardized pattern: deep copy the base scenario, override the target parameter, instantiate a fresh solver model per iteration, execute using the two-model signature, and collect and serialize results.
source_chunk_ids: 1. Structure of the REopt API · NatLabRockies:REopt_API Wiki_Django Models and Input Validation_0_57

claims.claim_text: The VoLL value may be a scalar or an 8,760-element (or sub-hourly) vector reflecting time-varying willingness-to-pay.

The `deepcopy`-override-solve-collect loop ensures each iteration operates on an independent scenario state, preventing cross-contamination of mutable dictionary references across parameter grid points.
source_chunk_ids: 1. Structure of the REopt API · NatLabRockies:REopt_API Wiki_Django Models and Input Validation_0_57

claims.claim_text: In v1/v2, each optimization job consists of four celery tasks (setup_scenario, one optimal and one bau run_jump_model, and process_results). In v3, there is only one celery task per optimization job.
source_chunk_ids: 1. Structure of the REopt API · NatLabRockies:REopt_API Wiki_Django Models and Input Validation_0_57
